title: The part that creates physical symptoms to get needs met indirectly
author: Kalesh

The part that wakes you up at 3am with a racing heart

htmlContent:
The part that wakes you up at 3am with a racing heart

You know that feeling. The one where your throat closes up before a meeting. The knot in your stomach that appears every time you have to ask for something. The tension headache that shows up like clockwork on Sunday evenings. I've lived with these sensations for years. And for a long time, I thought they were just random glitches in my body. Something wrong with me physically. Something I needed to fix with more sleep, less caffeine, better posture.

But here's the thing. These physical symptoms aren't random at all. They're coming from a specific part of you. A part that learned long ago that direct communication doesn't work. A part that discovered, through painful trial and error, that the only way to get needs met is through the body.

I call this the somatic messenger part. It's the part that creates physical symptoms to get needs met indirectly. And if you're reading this, I suspect you know this part intimately.

Think about it. When was the last time your body screamed something your mouth couldn't say? When was the last time you felt nauseous before a difficult conversation? When was the last time your shoulders locked up when someone asked for more than you could give?

This part isn't trying to hurt you. It's trying to protect you. It's trying to communicate in the only language that feels safe. Because somewhere along the way, you learned that saying "no" directly was dangerous. That asking for help would be met with rejection. That expressing your needs openly would lead to punishment or abandonment.

So this part took over. It found a way to get your needs met without you ever having to open your mouth. And honestly, it's pretty clever when you think about it.

The neuroscience behind the body's voice

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, spent decades studying how our nervous system communicates safety and danger. His work shows that our bodies are constantly scanning for cues of safety or threat. This happens below conscious awareness. Your body knows you're unsafe before your mind catches up.

Porges calls this process neuroception. It's your nervous system's ability to detect danger without involving your conscious brain. And when your neuroception picks up a threat, your body responds automatically. Heart rate increases. Breathing becomes shallow. Muscles tense up. Digestion slows down.

Now here's where it gets interesting. For people with complex trauma, this system gets calibrated differently. Your body learned that certain situations are dangerous even when your mind says they're fine. Your somatic messenger part remembers what your conscious mind has forgotten.

I remember working with a client who would get severe migraines every time she needed to ask her partner for help. She couldn't figure out why. She loved her partner. He was kind and responsive. But her body would shut down every time she had a legitimate need.

As we explored this, she remembered something. When she was five years old, she asked her mother for help with a scraped knee. Her mother was in the middle of something and snapped at her. "Can't you see I'm busy? Stop being so needy." That moment got coded into her nervous system. Asking for help = danger. And now, decades later, her body still responds as if that five-year-old is about to get yelled at.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, calls this "the wisdom of the body." Your body holds the complete memory of every experience, including the ones your mind has suppressed. And it will find a way to communicate those memories, even if it has to use physical symptoms to do it.

How the somatic messenger part develops

This part doesn't appear out of nowhere. It develops through specific patterns of relational trauma. Let me walk you through how this usually happens.

First, there's the environment where direct communication is punished. Maybe you grew up in a home where expressing needs led to ridicule. "Stop being so dramatic." "You're too sensitive." "Why can't you just be happy?" Your developing brain learned that saying what you need is dangerous.

Second, there's the environment where your needs were consistently ignored. You cried, and no one came. You asked for help, and received silence. Your body learned that direct communication is pointless. It doesn't work. So it found another way.

Third, there's the environment where your needs were used against you. Maybe a parent used your vulnerability as ammunition later. Maybe your pain was dismissed or pathologized. Your body learned that being seen is unsafe.

When these patterns repeat enough, the somatic messenger part takes over. It starts creating physical symptoms that force others to respond. A migraine that prevents you from going to that draining family gathering. Stomach pain that gets you out of a stressful work presentation. Chronic fatigue that finally makes you rest when your mind won't let you stop.

I know this sounds manipulative. But stay with me here. This part isn't being manipulative. It's being protective. It's using the only strategy that ever worked. And it's doing this automatically, without your conscious choice.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors. This book by Janina Fisher directly addresses how parts develop and how they communicate through the body.

Recognizing your somatic messenger part

So how do you know if this part is active in your life? Here are some signs I've noticed in myself and in the people I work with.

You get sick before important events. Not always. But often enough that you've started to notice a pattern. You have a big presentation coming up and suddenly you're in bed with a fever. You're supposed to visit family and your stomach rebels. Your body is creating a way out.

You experience chronic pain that doctors can't explain. Back pain, headaches, digestive issues. The medical tests come back normal, but the pain is very real. This is your body speaking a language that medicine hasn't fully learned to translate.

You have physical reactions to emotional triggers. Your throat closes up when you need to say no. Your chest tightens when someone asks for too much. Your hands go numb when you feel trapped. These are not random. They are specific messages from a part that needs you to listen.

Your symptoms appear in specific relational contexts. You feel fine when you're alone. But when you're around certain people, your body reacts. This is a huge clue. The symptom is not about the physical sensation itself. It's about the relationship dynamic that triggers the part.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty. This book by Jonice Webb explains how growing up with unmet emotional needs shapes the body's responses in adulthood.

Working with this part without fighting it

Here's what I've learned through my own recovery. You cannot bully this part into submission. You cannot meditate it away. You cannot positive-think your way out of physical symptoms that have been protecting you for decades.

What works is much simpler and much harder. You have to listen.

Start by noticing the symptom without trying to fix it. When your stomach clenches, just notice. When your headache starts, pay attention. Don't reach for the ibuprofen immediately. Don't try to breathe through it. Just observe.

Then, ask the symptom a question. Not in a demanding way. In a curious way. Say something like, "What are you trying to tell me?" or "What need is trying to be met here?" or "What would happen if you didn't have this symptom?"

I did this with a recurring pain in my lower back. Every time I had to set a boundary with someone, my back would lock up. One day, instead of stretching and ignoring it, I sat with it. I asked it what it needed. The answer came as a feeling, not words. It was a feeling of wanting to collapse. Of wanting to be small. Of wanting to disappear so I wouldn't have to say no.

That was a turning point for me. I realized my back pain was protecting me from the perceived danger of rejection. It was trying to make me invisible so I wouldn't have to risk someone being angry at me.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving. Pete Walker's work directly addresses how trauma creates parts that communicate through the body and how to work with them compassionately.

When you start listening, the symptoms often change. They might get louder at first. That's normal. Your part is testing you. It's checking to see if you're really going to listen or if you're just going to try to get rid of it again.

But over time, if you keep showing up with curiosity instead of resistance, something shifts. The symptom might soften. It might transform into something else. It might even disappear. Not because you fixed it, but because it no longer needs to scream for your attention.

The long road of integration

I won't pretend this is easy. Working with the somatic messenger part is some of the most challenging work I've done. Because it asks you to trust your body when you've been taught not to. It asks you to listen to symptoms when the world tells you to suppress them. It asks you to slow down when everything in your life is pushing you to speed up.

But here's what I've found on the other side. When you start listening to this part, you don't just lose the symptoms. You gain something. You gain access to needs you didn't know you had. You gain the ability to communicate directly instead of through your body. You gain the freedom to say no without getting sick.

This part has been working so hard for you. For years, maybe decades. It's been carrying a burden that was never yours to carry. It's been protecting you in the only way it knew how. And it deserves your gratitude, not your frustration.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace. Nedra Glover Tawwab's work helps you learn to communicate directly so your body doesn't have to do it for you.

The next time your body speaks, try listening. Try asking what it needs. Try thanking it for protecting you. You might be surprised by what you hear. And you might find that the symptom you've been fighting is actually the door to something deeper.

I'm still learning to listen. Some days I forget. Some days I reach for the ibuprofen before I check in. But I'm getting better. And that's enough. That's all any of us can do.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors - Janina Fisher's work on understanding how trauma fragments the self and how parts communicate through the body
  • Running on Empty - Jonice Webb's guide to understanding emotional neglect and how unmet needs shape adult patterns
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving - Pete Walker's resource for C-PTSD recovery and working with trauma responses
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace - Nedra Glover Tawwab's practical guide to direct communication and boundary setting
